This French "on" should thus definitely be translated as "they" - which is what the translation that I selected correctly does. The other translation kept the literal / usual meaning of "on" as "we" - which adds a serious error. In addition, the translation I selected is more idiomatic and flows well in English, contrary to the other one.

translation_a: The news of my arrest was a shock to my family. Marya Ivanovna had told my parents of my strange acquaintance with Pugachov so simply that, so far from being troubled about it, they often laughed at it with whole-hearted amusement. My father refused to believe that I could have been implicated in vile rebellion the aim of which was to overthrow the throne and exterminate the gentry. He closely questioned Savelyich. The old man did not conceal the fact that I had been to see Pugachov and that the villain had been kind to me; but he swore that he had not heard of any treason. My parents were reassured and waited impatiently for favorable news. Marya Ivanovna was very much alarmed but said nothing, for she was extremely modest and prudent.
translation_b: The news of my arrest shocked my entire family. Marya Ivanovna told my parents so simply about my strange acquaintance with Pugachev that it not only did not disturb them, but also made them often laugh from the bottom of their hearts. Batiushka did not want to believe that I could be involved in a vile rebellion, the purpose of which was the overthrow of the throne and the extermination of the noble family. He severely interrogated Savelich. The uncle did not hide the fact that the master was visiting Emelka Pugachev, and that the villain still complained about something; but he swore that he had never heard of any betrayal. The old people calmed down and began to look forward to favorable news. Marya Ivanovna was greatly alarmed, but kept silent, for she was gifted to the highest degree with modesty and caution.
comment: The first translation is more accurate. Let's have a look at the grammar. The source text makes it clear that Past Perfect should be used to identify the sequence of actions in the past: "The old man did not conceal the fact that I had been to see Pugachov and that the villain had been kind to me" but he swore that he had not heard of any treason" and it is used in the first translation. Moreover, the sentence mentioned above is incorrectly translated in the second translation, which makes it impossible to understand the whole passage. Vocabulary. translation_a: Even at the beginning, as soon as the prince entered the drawing room, he sat down as far as possible from the Chinese vase, with which Aglaya had so frightened him. Is it possible to believe that after Aglaya's words yesterday, some indelible conviction came into him, some amazing and impossible premonition that he would certainly break this vase tomorrow, no matter how he avoided it, no matter how he avoided trouble? But it was. In the course of the evening other strong but bright impressions began to flood into his soul; we already talked about this. He forgot his premonition. When he heard about Pavlishchev and Ivan Fyodorovich let him down and showed him again to Ivan Petrovich, he moved closer to the table and fell straight into an armchair beside a huge, beautiful Chinese vase, which stood on a pedestal, almost next to his elbow, a little behind.
translation_b: From the very beginning, as soon as the prince entered the drawing room, he sat down as far as possible from the Chinese vase, with which Aglaya had frightened him so. Can one possibly believe that, after Aglaya芒聙聶s words the day before, some sort of indelible conviction settled in him, some sort of astonishing and impossible premonition that the next day he would unfailingly break that vase, however far away he kept from it, however much he avoided the disaster? But it was so. In the course of the evening other strong but bright impressions began to flow into his soul; we have already spoken of that. He forgot his premonition. When he heard about Pavlishchev, and Ivan Fyodorovich brought him and introduced him again to Ivan Petrovich, he moved closer to the table and ended up right in the armchair next to the enormous, beautiful Chinese vase, which stood on a pedestal almost at his elbow, slightly behind him.
comment: I prefer the second translation for the following reasons: 1) According to the source text the premonition can't be described as "amazing" as it is given in the first translation. 2) There is a redundancy in the phrase "no matter how he avoided it, no matter how he avoided trouble" in the first translation while the exact same phrase in the second translation sounds just fine: "however far away he kept from it, however much he avoided the disaster" 3) The verb "fell straight into an armchair" from the first translation doesn't convey the meaning of the phrase from the source text while "ended up right in the armchair" from the second translation does it properly

translation_a: Empty. Quiet. The wind is there, behind the walls, as far away as that day when, shoulder to shoulder, two-one, we came out from below, from the corridors - if only it really was. I walked under some kind of stone arches, where the steps, hitting the damp arches, fell behind me - as if all the time another was walking behind me on my heels. Yellow - with red brick spots - the walls followed me through the dark square glasses of the windows, watched me open the melodious doors of barns, as I peered into corners, dead ends, nooks and crannies. The gate in the fence and the wasteland - a monument to the Great Bicentennial War: from the earth - bare stone ribs, yellow grinning jaws of the walls, an ancient furnace with a vertical pipe - forever a petrified ship among stone yellow and red brick bursts.
translation_b: Empty. Quiet. Wind outside, behind the walls, distant as the day when, shoulder to shoulder, two as one, we came out from below, from the corridors 芒聙聰if, indeed, this ever really happened. I walked beneath stone archways where my steps, resounding from the damp vaults, seemed to fall behind me, as if someone followed on my heels. Yellow walls with scars of red brick watched me through the dark glass squares of their windows, watched me open the singing doors of barns, peer into corners, dead ends, nooks, and crannies. A gate in the fence, and a desolate vacant lot芒聙聰memorial of the great Two Hundred Years' War. Rising from the earth-bare stony ribs, the yellow grinning jaws of walls, an ancient stove with a vertical chimney芒聙聰a ship forever petrified among the stony splashes of red and yellow brick.
comment: The second translation conveys the poetic component of the passage much more accurately. The author of the second translation seems to have been focused on the word choice more than the meaning these words convey. It can be seen in the phrase "Yellow - with red brick spots - the walls" which doesn't correspond to the meaning of the phrase in the source text while the same phrase in the second translation is just fine. The same thing with "as far away as that day" and "two-one" sound better in the second translation as "distant as the day" and "two as one".

translation_a: But now I allow myself to say that this was a cruel demand, at the same time I did not even think of it, I was only inexpressibly hurt. I was no longer in the position in which I was in my youth, thinking that everything in life is clear; After all, I came to faith because, besides faith, I found nothing, probably nothing, except death, so it was impossible to cast aside this faith, and I submitted. And I found in my soul a feeling that helped me bear it. It was a feeling of self-humiliation and humility. I resigned myself, swallowed this blood and body without blasphemous feeling, with a desire to believe, but the blow had already been dealt. And, knowing in advance what awaited me, I could no longer go another time.
translation_b: But now I let myself say it was a brutal requirement; then I didn芒聙聶t even think that, it was just inexpressibly painful for me. I was no longer in the situation I had been in my younger days, thinking that everything in life was clear; I had come to faith because apart from faith I had found nothing, really nothing but annihilation, so I couldn芒聙聶t reject this faith and I submitted. And I found a feeling in my soul that helped me to bear it. This was a feeling of self-abasement and humility. I humbled myself; I swallowed this flesh and blood without any feeling of blasphemy, with the desire to believe, but the blow had been struck. And knowing in advance what was waiting for me, I could no longer go a second time.
comment: The style of the source text is better conveyed in the second translation. The vocabulary in the second translation is better and more accurate. There is no tautology in the second translation as it is in the first translation ("self-humiliation and humility"). Grammar patterns are more natural in the second translation

translation_a: He did not finish; Ferdyshchenko quickly put a chair behind him, and the general, somewhat weak on his feet that afternoon, flopped down, or rather, fell on the chair, but this, however, did not embarrass him. He sat down directly opposite Nastasya Filippovna and with a pleasant grin, slowly and effectively, raised her fingers to his lips. In general, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His outward appearance, besides some slovenliness, was still quite decent, which he himself knew very well. He had happened before to be in very good society, from which he was finally excluded only two or three years ago. From the same period, he indulged too much without restraint in some of his weaknesses; but the dexterous and pleasant manner remained in him hitherto. Nastasya Filippovna seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, whom, of course, she knew from hearsay.
translation_b: He did not finish. Ferdyshchenko quickly offered him a chair from behind, and the general, somewhat weak in the legs after dinner, simply flopped or, better to say, collapsed into it; however, that did not embarrass him. He sat directly facing Nastasya Filippovna and, with a pleasant little grimace, slowly and dramatically brought her fingers to his lips. On the whole, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His appearance, apart from a certain slovenliness, was still quite decent, as he knew very well himself. In the past he had occasionally been received in very good society, from which he had been definitively excluded only two or three years ago. It was then that he gave himself over all too unrestrainedly to some of his weaknesses; but he still retained his adroit and pleasant manner. Nastasya Filippovna, it seemed, was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, of whom she knew, of course, by hearsay.
comment: Both translations are quite good, but I prefer the second translation to the first one. The vocabulary in the second translation is more accurate: "weak in the legs" over "weak on his feet", "brought her fingers to his lips" over "raised her fingers to his lips", "was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich" over "seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance". Such small details lead to the understanding of the whole text.

translation_a: But it turned out that you are just a dignitary, ignorant and stupid, like all the dignitaries who were before you and will be after you. And, like all dignitaries, you are a tyrant. translation_a: Lebedev drew himself up. It is true that he was sometimes even too naive and importunate in his curiosity; but at the same time he was a rather cunning and sinuous person, and in some cases even too insidiously silent; By continuous repulsions, the prince almost prepared an enemy for himself in him. But the prince repulsed him not because he despised him, but because the topic of his curiosity was delicate. Several days ago, the prince looked at some of his dreams as a crime, but Lukyan Timofeich took the prince芒聙聶s refusals for nothing more than personal disgust and distrust towards himself, went away with a wounded heart and was jealous of the prince, not only Kolya and Keller, but even his own daughter. , Vera Lukyanovna. Even at that very moment, perhaps, he could and wished to sincerely inform the prince of one piece of news that was extremely interesting for the prince, but he gloomily fell silent and did not tell him.
translation_b: Lebedev assumed a dignified air. It is true that he was sometimes even too na脙炉ve and importunate in his curiosity; but at the same time he was a rather cunning and devious man, and on certain occasions even too insidiously taciturn; by constantly rebuffing him, the prince had almost prepared in him an enemy for himself. But the prince rebuffed him not because he despised him, but because the theme of his curiosity was a delicate one. Only a few days ago the prince had looked upon some of his dreams as upon a crime, but Lukyan Timofeich had taken the prince芒聙聶s retorts as personal revulsion and suspicion towards himself, had gone away with a wounded heart, and was jealous not only of Kolya and Keller, but even of his own daughter, Vera Lukyanovna. Even at that very moment, he could have informed the prince of a certain piece of news interesting for him in the highest degree, and may have sincerely wished to, but he fell gloomily silent and did not inform him.
comment: The second translation is better for several reasons. The phrase "was jealous not only of Kolya and Keller, but even of his own daughter, Vera Lukyanovna" in the second translation is the exact accurate translation of the sentence in the source text while the phrase "was jealous of the prince, not only Kolya and Keller, but even his own daughter. , Vera Lukyanovna" contains a mistake that results in the misunderstanding of the meaning of the sentence as a whole. The sentence "he could have informed the prince of a certain piece of news interesting for him in the highest degree, and may have sincerely wished to" in the second translation not only is natural but also is grammatically correct while "he could and wished to sincerely inform the prince of one piece of news" isn't at all good enough to use.

translation_a: Since he often jumped from one thing to another and forgot what he had begun to say, the prince kept still so as to let him speak everything out. But all the same it was extremely unclear whether the letters had gone through him or through Vera. translation_b: Since he often strayed from one to the other and forgot what he began to talk about, the prince fell silent to let him speak. But all the same, it was extremely unclear: did the letters go through him or through Vera? If he himself assured that "to Rogozhin is the same as to Nastasya Filippovna," then it means, rather, that they did not go through him, if only there were letters. The incident, how the letter now came to him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely it was necessary to assume that he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it with some intention to Lizaveta Prokofievna. comment: The first translation is a better one for various reasons: Grammar: according to the source text Past perfect should be used to identify a series of actions that happened before other actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation Vocabulary: "And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him" in the first translation is more accurate and conveys the meaning better than "The incident, how the letter now came to him" in the second translation. The same thing with "carried it off on the sly" (1st translation) over "quietly stole it". Moreover, there is a tautology in the second translation: "he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it". The last sentence in the second translation isn't accurate and doesn't sum up the idea presented in the passage, which makes the sentence in the first translation better.

translation_a: As a soccer player Volodya represented a complete opposite to Getzke. Volodya was a professional sportsman芒聙聯the other was a professional player. Important to Volodya was the general course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome芒聙聯Getzke strove only to show his own skill. He was an old, experienced player, not intending to maintain the honor of the team: he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sort of sports organization because he had compromised himself by moves from club to club for money. They forbade him to participate in matches for the play-off of a championship. They invited him only for friendly games, demonstration matches and trips to other countries. Skill united in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He was suspicious of the players芒聙聯both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would drive in the balls on any team. The rest was not important to him. He was a hack.
translation_b: As a football player, Volodya was the exact opposite of Getska. Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the overall course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome - Getsuke sought only to show his art. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honor of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He was banned from participating in championship matches. He was invited only to a friendly game, exhibition matches and trips to other countries. Art was combined in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents. He knew that he would score goals for any team. The rest didn't matter to him. He was a hack.
comment: There is no doubt that the first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) There is a mistake in spelling the name of the character in the second translation. It should be "Getzke" while the author of the second translation writes "Getska" or even "Getsuke". 2) According to the source text a story is told about two characters. The second sentence of the passage should give information about two different people while there is only one person mentioned in the second sentence of the 2nd translation: "Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player" 3) The author of the second translation uses a word-by-word translation of the sentence "He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents" which cannot be used here
